## [00:00 - 03:00]

So in this lesson we're going to look at the business models of direct to consumer versus distribution or a combination of direct to consumer and distribution. They all work for different people at different times, different business at different times but we're going to have a look in today's market, what are the pros and cons? This topic is actually really important to look at now because the world has gone through a big change over the last couple of years and it's continuously changing, it's only changing faster if anything and the power is really going back to the consumers and the brands I believe and the people in the middle, all other than probably Amazon are really in a very tricky position and these are distributors. So originally if you were a brand and you had a product you wanted to get it to the market you absolutely had to use distributors and retailers. Obviously we all know this has changed a lot over the last 10 years and now it's possible for a brand to skip everyone and just go straight to the end user, just go straight to that customer, start a relationship with that customer and I really believe in the next couple of years we're going to see the value in business is really the relationship. So whoever owns that relationship with the end user with the customer is going to be the winner. So for a brand you want to own as much of that relationship I think as possible but as you scale and as you get bigger probably around now to as you continue to scale you'll see opportunities come up and I think it's very easy for us to go back in the old way thinking like I want my product to be everywhere, I've got to go through distribution, I've got to go get all the big retailers and I'm just not 100% sure that's the case. It's kind of like building your business on a swamp that's continuously moving, like you're not really in control where if there could be some good partners out there that are strategic and that can help you scale your business but at the same time a lot of the time people are just gathering sales that you'll have to make yourself anyway. So if you're going to the market with your own marketing and you're being successful try scale that and try and solve your old problems over making your problems somebody else's problems and what I mean by saying that is that don't expect a single retailer or a single distributor that if we get them and they put us in I don't know big box store X we're going to sell heaps of units and everything's going to be okay I just I don't see that happening. Don't use Amazon as your one channel to market or as your main channel to market because at the end of the day they own that relationship you don't own that relationship they are the ones that are going to upsell to your customers not you they have all your data so anything you can these can be parts of your strategy but they can't be any all of your strategy so if you can prioritize the direct to consumer first and then tack on these things where

## [03:00 - 06:00]

they make strategic sense I believe in businesses now and moving forward that's where the value is going to line that's where success will lie. So if a quad lock as we all grow and became more and more popular and more and more people want to sell quad locks so we thought we'll just get a distributor we get one or two distributors then we got more distributors then we got bigger distributors then we went to sort of the biggest and best distributors in each category that we also in some of the categories that we were selling in and at a point some of them provide value and a lot of them weren't providing value and so we decided you know the real way to scale this business is to go direct to the customer really foster that relationship look after them make sure they get everything they need have a clear channel of communication understand them better than any distributor or retail is going to understand them keep the margin from the sale and reinvest that margin into aggressive marketing into growing your brand into growing your customer base and then you're in the position where you can come out with another product offer to the same customers they think quad lock they go you get it from quad lock they don't think Nike let's go to Foot Locker and that's how I see businesses scaling moving forward. So one of the problems we found with distribution is that yeah they can get you sales but and it means it's not necessarily costing you marketing dollars some of those sales probably not as many as anyone will promise you but the problem there is all you're doing is subsidizing marketing dollars for margin for the retail and distributor so there's still value disappearing from this transaction of a product going to a customer. So if a distributor or retailer are putting this product to that customer and you could be putting that product to that customer and you could be either spending the money on marketing or sacrificing that money in the middle of it margin to pay the distributor in the retailer I'd rather be spending than on marketing because not only do you get product X to customer X you also get the product X to customer X with all the people along the way you've taught about your brand they know your brand you become more of a household name and that is much more beneficial for the longevity of a brand and the growth of a brand. So probably another key takeaway from everything I've just said here is that this model doesn't suit all products and all brands some brands need to be completely mass market they need to grow themselves and they need to go to distribution and they need to get pumped through distribution and big retailers to be a successor I totally understand that but I still think at a certain point in time if you can keep some of that customer basis for yourself that you are you know really involved with you're getting reviews from you're really owning at least part of the relationship there's still a lot of value in that for a brand no matter what but I think based on the nature of this course and e-commerce I think most of the people watching
